Home / Mega-Cap / GOOGL
GOOGL

Alphabet's AI Everything Strategy: The Talent Bleed and the $4.22T Reality Check

Two AI researchers walked and $250B in market cap evaporated. But behind the panic, Alphabet just posted $109.9B in Q1 revenue, Cloud is growing 63% YoY, and Gemini Pro 3.1 is undercutting everyone on price.

Jun 23, 2026 4 min read

Two AI researchers walked out of Google last week and $250 billion vanished. Not a cyberattack. Not an earnings miss. Two resignations — a Gemini co-lead and Nobel laureate John Jumper from DeepMind, who bolted to Anthropic — and the market hit eject on $GOOGL like the AI thesis just got debunked. Panic looks exactly like this.

But here's what the panic missed: Jumper won a Nobel for AlphaFold. Losing him stings. But if you sold because two people quit, you weren't reading the income statement. And the income statement is screaming.

The Numbers Don't Lie

MetricValue
Market Cap$4.22T
Revenue (TTM)$422.5B
Q1 2026 Revenue$109.9B (+21.8% YoY)
Cloud Q1 Revenue$20B (+63% YoY)
Cloud Backlog$460B
Trailing P/E26.4
Forward P/E23.78
FY2026 Capex$185-190B
Gross Margin60.37%
Operating Margin36.12%
YTD Return+10.02%

Cloud Is Eating Everyone's Lunch

For years Google Cloud was the punchline — distant third place nobody took seriously. That joke's dead. Cloud revenue hit $20 billion in Q1, growing 63% year-over-year. Faster than AWS. Faster than Azure. The backlog: $460 billion in signed contracts — future revenue already locked. The Palantir-Google Cloud deal is pulling defense and enterprise AI workloads onto Google infrastructure at a scale nobody saw coming.

Gemini Pro 3.1: Google Just Became the Price Setter

On June 21, Google dropped Gemini Pro 3.1 and undercut OpenAI and Anthropic on cost per token — by margins that'll make competitors' CFOs lose sleep. Combined with Google I/O 2026 launches — Gemini 3.5 Flash for speed, Gemini Omni for multimodal dominance, and Gemini Spark, a life-planning AI agent — Google isn't just playing in AI anymore. It's writing the pricing handbook. When you own the TPU infrastructure, you fight a margin war nobody else can win.

The Capex Monster That Buffett Backed

Sundar Pichai told Wall Street capex is landing at $185-190 billion in 2026. That number makes people dizzy — until you remember they raised $80-85 billion in a stock offering that included a $10 billion check from Berkshire Hathaway. Buffett's team doesn't write ten-figure checks for vibes. Earnings grew 82%. Margins sit at 36.12%. Fifty-three analysts at Strong Buy. This isn't spending — it's laying the rails the AI economy runs on for the next decade.

Waymo: The $220M Power Move

While everyone obsesses over Tesla's robotaxi promises, Waymo quietly expanded 20%+, launched cheaper robotaxis in LA, and dropped $220 million to buy Apple's self-driving proving ground. Apple spent a decade trying to build what Waymo just bought at auction. That's not a footnote — that's monopoly posture in a market projected to be worth trillions.

$GOOGL sits 15% off its $408 highs — the 52-week range spans $166 to $408. Beta of 1.24 means swings are guaranteed. But at 23.78x forward earnings with Cloud accelerating faster than any competitor, AI pricing power locked in through TPU cost advantages, and Waymo emerging as a standalone juggernaut, the sell-off on talent departures looks less like analysis and more like a gift-wrapped entry point.
